recap: Plot cumulative frequencies against upper class boundaries. Median = value at n/2; Q1 = value at n/4; Q3 = value at 3n/4. IQR = Q3 - Q1. A box plot shows min, Q1, median, Q3, max.

Q1.question: A frequency table has groups 0-10, 10-20, 20-30 with frequencies 8, 15, 7. Write the cumulative frequencies.

Q2.question: From a cumulative frequency curve for 60 students, the median is at the 30th value = 52 marks, Q1 is at the 15th value = 42 marks, Q3 is at the 45th value = 61 marks. Find the IQR.

Q3.question: 80 people were surveyed on how long they waited. From the curve: Q1 = 12 mins, median = 20 mins, Q3 = 30 mins. The minimum was 2 mins and maximum was 45 mins. Describe the five number summary for a box plot.

steps: The data is slightly positively skewed (median is closer to Q1 than Q3).
solution.final_answer: Min=2, Q1=12, Median=20, Q3=30, Max=45; IQR=18

Q4.question: From the cumulative frequency curve for 100 values, estimate the number of values greater than 35 if the cumulative frequency at 35 is 72.

Q5.question: Class A (50 students): median 55, IQR 10. Class B (50 students): median 62, IQR 22. Compare the two distributions.

steps: Class B has a higher median (62 vs 55), so Class B performed better on average.
Class A has a smaller IQR (10 vs 22), so Class A's results are more consistent.
Class B's larger IQR means scores are more spread out — a wider range of ability.
Overall: Class B achieved higher marks but with less consistency than Class A.
solution.final_answer: Class B scored higher on average (median 62 vs 55) but Class A was more consistent (IQR 10 vs 22)

summary: Plot cumulative frequencies at upper class boundaries. Start the curve at zero at the lower boundary of the first class.
Median at n/2, Q1 at n/4, Q3 at 3n/4. IQR = Q3 - Q1 = spread of the middle 50%.
A box plot uses: minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum. The box covers Q1 to Q3.
When comparing two distributions, comment on both average (median) and spread (IQR).
